eyebrow: For symptoms mixed with theories
headline: A problem brief separating observed behavior, assumed cause, and desired change.
subheadline: Capture one concrete example, what actually happened, the current cause theory, and the outcome the work needs to restore.
chatStarter: I keep mixing what I observed with what I think caused it. I need a brief that separates them.
whyItWorks: It promises one usable diagnostic artifact with fields that prevent a theory from masquerading as evidence.

eyebrow: Keep closed choices closed responsibly
headline: A rejected-options appendix showing what could legitimately reopen each choice.
subheadline: Record the rejected option, decisive reason, accepted tradeoff, and new evidence that would earn another review.
chatStarter: The same rejected options keep returning because nobody recorded what could make them relevant again.
whyItWorks: The appendix preserves optionality without letting old debates reopen for no new reason.

eyebrow: Not every limit has equal authority
headline: A constraint hierarchy showing which limit outranks the others.
subheadline: Order deadline, budget, capacity, risk, and maintenance by which one is allowed to decide when they conflict.
chatStarter: Our constraints point in different directions, and I need to know which one wins when they conflict.
whyItWorks: A ranked artifact resolves the common problem of treating every stated constraint as equally decisive.

eyebrow: Give each question its real due date
headline: A decision calendar showing when each open question actually becomes due.
subheadline: Place each question beside the event, dependency, or cost that makes an answer necessary—not merely desirable.
chatStarter: Everything feels due now, but these decisions cannot all become consequential at the same time.
whyItWorks: The calendar turns vague urgency into a visible sequence tied to real consequences.

eyebrow: Keep advice inside its evidence
headline: A recommendation boundary card: applies here, fails there, review when.
subheadline: State the cases the advice covers, the condition that invalidates it, and the date or signal for review.
chatStarter: The recommendation is useful, but I need a compact record of where it stops applying.
whyItWorks: The card makes honest limits portable into the work instead of leaving them in conversation.

eyebrow: Ask only for evidence that can change the answer
headline: An evidence request list separating logs, examples, access, and unanswered decisions.
subheadline: List only the material needed to diagnose, compare, or approve the current choice, grouped by who can provide it.
chatStarter: We keep asking for more information without knowing which evidence could actually change the recommendation.
whyItWorks: A bounded request list reduces preparation while making the missing evidence operationally clear.

eyebrow: Every item earns a visible status
headline: A backlog triage sheet explaining why each item moves, waits, or leaves.
subheadline: Sort items by consequence, dependency, and evidence, then record the reason behind active, parked, and removed work.
chatStarter: Our backlog has priorities, but nobody can explain why an item is active, waiting, or still present.
whyItWorks: It promises a maintainable prioritization artifact rather than another unreasoned ranking.

eyebrow: A short route through live work
headline: A one-week route with one outcome, one dependency, and one review.
subheadline: Name the result the week should produce, the dependency that can block it, and the evidence the review will inspect.
chatStarter: I need a route for this week that protects one outcome and acknowledges the dependency already threatening it.
whyItWorks: The route is compact, time-bound, and specific enough to review without becoming a full roadmap.

eyebrow: Unlock work in the right sequence
headline: A dependency release order showing which choice unlocks the rest.
subheadline: Map blocked work to its shared dependencies, then order the decisions by how much useful work each one releases.
chatStarter: Several tasks are blocked, and I suspect one unresolved choice is holding most of them together.
whyItWorks: The output turns a crowded dependency map into an actionable release sequence.

eyebrow: Show what each cut protects and costs
headline: A scope tension table: outcome protected, feature removed, consequence accepted.
subheadline: Place each proposed cut beside the customer outcome it preserves and the limitation the team agrees to accept.
chatStarter: We need to cut scope, but I want every removal tied to what the release still promises.
whyItWorks: It makes scope reduction traceable to outcome and consequence rather than arbitrary feature removal.

eyebrow: Evidence instead of status performance
headline: A progress proof snapshot your team can review without another status meeting.
subheadline: Capture what changed, the artifact or observation proving it, and the decision that the evidence creates next.
chatStarter: The team reports activity every week, but we need a compact view of what actually changed.
whyItWorks: The snapshot gives progress an inspectable form while keeping the output lighter than another meeting.

eyebrow: Give edge cases an operating home
headline: A workflow exception register with frequency, owner, and recovery route.
subheadline: List real exceptions, how often they occur, who decides, and the path that returns each case to useful work.
chatStarter: Our normal workflow is documented, but exceptions still become improvised work every time.
whyItWorks: The register makes exceptions manageable without pretending the routine path can absorb them all.

eyebrow: Test the handoff beyond its happy path
headline: An integration test card covering normal transfer, missing data, and duplicates.
subheadline: Define expected behavior for one valid record, one required-field failure, and one repeated event before connecting more cases.
chatStarter: The integration passes the happy path, but missing fields and repeated events are still undefined.
whyItWorks: Three concrete cases make integration reliability testable without implying exhaustive coverage.

eyebrow: Completion includes confirmed arrival
headline: A handoff receipt showing what arrived, what is missing, and who confirms.
subheadline: Record the expected input, received material, missing context, and person responsible for accepting or returning the handoff.
chatStarter: Work is being sent onward, but nobody confirms whether the next person received enough to continue.
whyItWorks: A receipt closes the gap between sending work and knowing the next person can use it.

eyebrow: Know which record has the final word
headline: A data authority table: source, editor, consumer, and final owner.
subheadline: For each important field, identify where it originates, who may change it, which systems use it, and who resolves conflict.
chatStarter: Several systems hold the same fields, and nobody knows which record wins when values disagree.
whyItWorks: The table makes source-of-truth decisions field-specific instead of leaving authority at the tool level.

eyebrow: Leaving needs an operating sequence
headline: A tool retirement plan covering export, ownership transfer, and continuity checks.
subheadline: Sequence data export, replacement ownership, integration changes, access removal, and a check that active work still continues.
chatStarter: We know this tool should leave, but closing it safely touches data, access, billing, and active work.
whyItWorks: It turns a tool decision into a concrete continuity plan instead of a premature cancellation.

eyebrow: One compact launch gate
headline: A release readiness card with pass condition, rollback point, and observer.
subheadline: Name the behavior required to launch, the state safe enough to restore, and the person watching the first consequence.
chatStarter: We need one compact launch gate that says what must pass, how we recover, and who watches.
whyItWorks: The card joins readiness, recovery, and observation without claiming to replace a full launch process.

eyebrow: Locate the change between working and broken
headline: A failure timeline connecting the last working state to the first broken one.
subheadline: Place changes, deployments, inputs, and observed behavior in sequence until the investigation has a bounded comparison window.
chatStarter: It worked before and fails now, but our account of what changed is scattered across several places.
whyItWorks: The timeline narrows diagnosis to an evidence window without promising an instant root cause.

eyebrow: Know what moves the number
headline: An estimate range table showing what narrows, widens, or invalidates the number.
subheadline: List the evidence that would remove uncertainty, add work, or make the current range unsuitable enough to replace.
chatStarter: I have an estimate range, but I need to know which new information would move or invalidate it.
whyItWorks: The table makes estimate revision explicit without pretending uncertainty can be compressed into one fixed number.

eyebrow: Launch creates operating responsibilities
headline: A post-launch ownership sheet for monitoring, support, exceptions, and escalation.
subheadline: Assign who watches the system, answers user problems, handles abnormal cases, and decides when an issue escalates.
chatStarter: The system can launch, but nobody has accepted the work that begins the morning after.
whyItWorks: It makes the operating handoff visible before launch quietly creates unowned work.

eyebrow: Watch only risks capable of changing action
headline: A risk watchlist ranked by consequence, earliest signal, and response owner.
subheadline: Keep the risks that can alter scope, timing, or direction, then attach the first observable warning and responsible person.
chatStarter: Our risk list is long, but it does not say which warning should change what we do.
whyItWorks: The watchlist connects risk to signal and ownership, making it usable rather than merely comprehensive.

eyebrow: Hand off the result, not just the task
headline: A developer acceptance packet: user behavior, limits, examples, and done check.
subheadline: Describe the change users need, important constraints, representative examples, and the observable check that accepts the work.
chatStarter: A developer can implement this, but I need the expected result defined well enough to accept it.
whyItWorks: The packet gives implementation a verifiable destination without pretending to specify every technical detail.

eyebrow: Repair the model, then test transfer
headline: A misconception repair plan: concept, counterexample, practice, and transfer check.
subheadline: Name the mistaken model, confront it with one counterexample, practice the corrected principle, and test it in a new case.
chatStarter: I fixed the answer, but I think the same misconception will return in a different form.
whyItWorks: The plan makes learning corrective and transferable instead of stopping at one repaired output.

eyebrow: Review the judgment the project needs
headline: A review rubric tied to the exact judgment your project needs next.
subheadline: Choose the criteria that reveal whether the work demonstrates the next required judgment, not every possible quality dimension.
chatStarter: I need feedback on whether this work shows the judgment required for the next project step.
whyItWorks: The rubric focuses feedback on one project-relevant capability rather than producing a generic evaluation.

eyebrow: Carry the principle beyond one answer
headline: A reusable pattern card: problem shape, principle, boundary, and next variation.
subheadline: Capture how to recognize the pattern, why the principle fits, where it stops, and one different case to try next.
chatStarter: I understand this solution, but I want a compact way to recognize and test the pattern elsewhere.
whyItWorks: The card converts an isolated answer into a bounded, testable piece of reusable judgment.

eyebrow: Keep the principle behind the correction
headline: A feedback translation note: correction, underlying principle, and next use.
subheadline: Record what should change now, the reasoning behind it, and the next situation where the same principle deserves testing.
chatStarter: I can apply this correction, but I do not want the principle behind it to disappear afterward.
whyItWorks: The note preserves immediate usefulness while extending feedback into the next relevant decision.

eyebrow: Define when this guidance has done its job
headline: A skill exit test defining when the same guidance is no longer needed.
subheadline: Specify the decision, explanation, application, and variation you should handle independently before closing this learning focus.
chatStarter: I want a clear test for when I can make this kind of decision without the same help.
whyItWorks: The artifact makes independence observable and keeps mentorship from implying permanent reliance.

eyebrow: Ask beyond the feature list
headline: A vendor question sheet exposing ownership, exit cost, failure, and support.
subheadline: Ask who controls the data, how leaving works, what happens during failure, and which support path owns resolution.
chatStarter: The vendor answered every feature question, but ownership, failure, and exit are still vague.
whyItWorks: The sheet equips the client to examine operating risk before a vendor choice becomes dependency.

eyebrow: Translate technology into accountable change
headline: A proposal translation table: change, cost, risk, owner, and acceptance.
subheadline: Place each technical proposal beside the working change it creates, its cost and risk, responsible person, and approval condition.
chatStarter: The proposal explains the technology, but I need a table that makes each change approvable.
whyItWorks: The table gives nontechnical approval a repeatable artifact without diluting the technical content.

eyebrow: One decision, clear Monday work
headline: A role-by-role action sheet translating one technical decision into Monday work.
subheadline: For each affected role, state what changes, what stays, what they need first, and which result comes back for review.
chatStarter: The decision is made, but product, operations, and development still need different next actions.
whyItWorks: The sheet bridges decision and coordinated work without promising a universal artifact for unrelated situations.
